hook.text: Drug A beats Drug B in men. Drug A beats Drug B in women. But Drug B beats Drug A overall. How?

buildup.text: Simpson's paradox occurs when a trend in subgroups reverses when the groups are combined.

discovery.text: The reversal happens because group sizes differ. More men got Drug B, and men had higher recovery rates.

twist.text: UC Berkeley was sued for gender bias. Women were rejected more overall but accepted more in each department.

climax.text: Women applied to harder departments. Aggregating hid the true story. The paradox isn't rare — it's everywhere.

punchline.text: The whole can tell a different story than its parts. Always look deeper.

quiz.question: What causes Simpson's paradox?
options: Unequal group sizes cause a trend in subgroups to reverse when data is combined
Random chance always produces contradictory results
Large sample sizes create statistical errors

hook.text: Countries with more chocolate consumption win more Nobel Prizes. Should you eat chocolate to get smarter?

buildup.text: The ecological fallacy assumes that patterns in groups apply to individuals within those groups.

discovery.text: Wealthy countries buy more chocolate and fund more research. The link is wealth, not cocoa improving brains.

twist.text: Entire research fields have been misled by ecological correlations. Regional data can't prove individual causation.

climax.text: What's true for the average of a group isn't necessarily true for any member of that group.

punchline.text: Groups and individuals follow different rules. Don't confuse the two.

quiz.question: What is the ecological fallacy?
options: Assuming that patterns observed in groups apply to individual members of those groups
Making predictions about ecology from economic data
Using too-small sample sizes in environmental studies

hook.text: Move a patient from the 'mild' group to the 'severe' group. Both groups' survival rates improve. No one got better.

buildup.text: Will Rogers joked that Oklahomans moving to California raised the average IQ of both states.

discovery.text: When the worst member of one group moves to another, they lower that group's worst but raise the first group's average.

twist.text: Cancer staging improvements cause this paradox. Better diagnosis reclassifies patients, making all stages look improved.

climax.text: Stage migration means cancer survival statistics can appear to improve even with zero actual treatment progress.

punchline.text: Reclassify the data and everyone looks better. Nothing actually changed.

quiz.question: How does stage migration cause the Will Rogers paradox in cancer statistics?
options: Better diagnosis reclassifies patients between stages, making survival rates in all stages appear to improve
New treatments improve every patient equally
Patients are randomly reassigned between stages

hook.text: Two statisticians analyze the same data on weight gain. They reach opposite conclusions. Both are correct.

buildup.text: Students eat at two dining halls. Statistician A compares weight change. Statistician B adjusts for starting weight.

discovery.text: Without adjustment, no difference. Adjusting for baseline, Hall B adds more weight. Same data, different answers.

twist.text: The paradox arises because adjusting for a pre-treatment variable changes what 'the effect' means.

climax.text: Lord's paradox has confused researchers since 1967. It reveals the deep ambiguity of 'controlling for' variables.

punchline.text: Same data. Different questions. Different answers. Both legitimate.

quiz.question: What does Lord's paradox demonstrate?
options: Adjusting for baseline variables can change the conclusion — the 'right' answer depends on the question asked
Statistical software always produces errors
Larger samples always resolve conflicting results

hook.text: To estimate a baseball player's batting average, look at the temperature in Tokyo. Seriously.

buildup.text: Stein showed that estimating three or more means simultaneously improves when you shrink them toward the center.

discovery.text: The James-Stein estimator beats individual averages even when the variables are completely unrelated.

twist.text: It works because extreme values are likely overestimates. Pulling toward the center corrects for randomness.

climax.text: The paradox challenged a fundamental belief: that the best estimate of each mean is its own sample mean.

punchline.text: Borrow strength from unrelated data. The group lifts every estimate.

quiz.question: Why does the James-Stein estimator outperform individual sample means?
options: Shrinking extreme estimates toward the grand mean corrects for random overestimation
It uses a larger sample size
It only works with baseball statistics

hook.text: In a room of just 23 people, there's a 50% chance two share a birthday. In 70 people, it's 99.9%.

buildup.text: People compare their birthday to the 364 other days. But the puzzle counts all possible pairs.

discovery.text: With 23 people, there are 253 pairs. Each pair has a small chance of matching, but 253 tries add up fast.

twist.text: People guess you need 183 people for 50%. Their intuition is off by a factor of 8. Combinatorics is counter-intuitive.

climax.text: The birthday problem appears in cryptography: hash collisions follow the same math.

punchline.text: 23 people, 253 pairs, 50% match. Pairs grow much faster than people.

quiz.question: Why does the birthday problem require only 23 people for a 50% match?
options: 23 people create 253 pairs — the many pair comparisons make a match likely
Most people are born in the same month
The calculation only works with random guessing

hook.text: You pick door 1. The host opens door 3, revealing a goat. Should you switch to door 2? Yes — always.

buildup.text: You initially had a 1/3 chance of being right. The other two doors held 2/3 of the probability.

discovery.text: The host always reveals a goat. The 2/3 probability collapses onto the remaining door. Switching wins 2/3 of the time.

twist.text: Nearly 1,000 PhD holders wrote to Marilyn vos Savant saying she was wrong. She was right. They were fooled.

climax.text: The key insight: the host's action gives you information. Not switching ignores free information.

punchline.text: Switch doors. The host just told you where the car probably is.

quiz.question: Why does switching doors in the Monty Hall problem improve your odds?
options: The host's reveal concentrates the 2/3 probability onto the remaining unchosen door
The car is always behind the last door
Random chance favors switching

hook.text: Roulette hits black 10 times in a row. Everyone bets red. But the wheel doesn't remember.

buildup.text: The gambler's fallacy is the belief that past random events affect future probabilities.

discovery.text: Each spin is independent. The probability of black is always 18/37, no matter what came before.

twist.text: In 1913 at Monte Carlo, black hit 26 times in a row. Bettors lost millions betting red. The casino got richer.

climax.text: The fallacy confuses the probability of a sequence with the probability of the next event. They're different.

punchline.text: The coin doesn't remember. Neither should you.

quiz.question: What is the gambler's fallacy?
options: Believing past random outcomes affect the probability of future independent events
Thinking you can predict the future with statistics
Betting too much on a single outcome

hook.text: Buses arrive every 10 minutes on average. You arrive at a random time. Your expected wait? Not 5 minutes.

buildup.text: You're more likely to arrive during a long gap between buses than a short one. Long intervals are easier to land in.

discovery.text: Your average wait is longer than half the average interval. If gaps vary, you'll typically wait more than 5 minutes.

twist.text: The paradox extends: your co-authors publish more and your followers have more followers. Popularity bias.

climax.text: The inspection paradox appears in class sizes, pregnancy durations, and network science. It's everywhere.

punchline.text: You oversample the long intervals just by showing up. Size biases observation.

quiz.question: Why is your expected bus wait longer than half the average interval?
options: You're more likely to arrive during a long gap between buses — long intervals are oversampled
Bus schedules are always wrong
Buses come in clusters

hook.text: A family has two children. One is a boy. What's the probability the other is also a boy? Not 1/2.

buildup.text: Two children create four equally likely outcomes: BB, BG, GB, GG. Knowing one is a boy eliminates only GG.

discovery.text: Three outcomes remain: BB, BG, GB. Only one has two boys. The probability is 1/3, not 1/2.

twist.text: But if you know the older child is a boy, it's 1/2. How you learn the information changes the probability.

climax.text: The paradox reveals that probability depends on the exact question asked. Phrasing is everything.

punchline.text: 'One is a boy' and 'the first is a boy' give different answers.

quiz.question: In the boy or girl paradox, why is the answer 1/3 instead of 1/2?
options: Knowing 'at least one is a boy' leaves three equally likely outcomes, and only one is two boys
Boys are statistically rarer than girls
The paradox only works with large families

hook.text: WWII planes returned with bullet holes on the wings. Engineers wanted to armor them. Wald said no.

buildup.text: The planes you see survived. The ones hit in the engine never came back. You're studying the survivors, not the victims.

discovery.text: Armor the places with no bullet holes — those are the hits that were fatal. The missing data tells the real story.

twist.text: Survivorship bias explains why old buildings seem sturdier: the weak ones already collapsed. Only the strong survived.

climax.text: Self-help books interview successful founders. Thousands who did the same and failed aren't interviewed.

punchline.text: The dead don't report. Look at what's missing, not what survived.

quiz.question: Why did Abraham Wald recommend armoring parts of planes without bullet holes?
options: Planes hit in those areas never returned — the surviving planes showed non-fatal damage only
Bullets always miss the same spots
The engineers already armored the visible damage

hook.text: In hospital data, two unrelated diseases appear negatively correlated. Neither causes the other. What's happening?

buildup.text: Berkson's paradox occurs when you condition on a collider — a variable caused by both factors you're studying.

discovery.text: Hospital admission is the collider. Admitted for either disease, having one makes the other seem rarer.

twist.text: The dating 'attractiveness vs. niceness' trade-off may be Berkson's paradox. You filter on the sum.

climax.text: Conditioning on colliders creates spurious correlations. Causal diagrams help you spot them before they mislead.

punchline.text: Select on the sum and the parts look opposed. The filter creates the illusion.

quiz.question: What causes Berkson's paradox?
options: Conditioning on a collider variable that's caused by both factors, creating a spurious correlation
Having too few patients in the study
Using the wrong statistical test

hook.text: A student scores 98 on a test. Next test: 85. Did they get lazier? Probably not. It's regression to the mean.

buildup.text: Extreme results have a luck component. On the next measurement, luck is unlikely to be equally extreme.

discovery.text: Francis Galton noticed tall parents had children closer to average height. Extreme values pull back toward the center.

twist.text: Sports Illustrated cover jinx, sophomore slumps, and 'miracle cures' are all regression to the mean in disguise.

climax.text: Without understanding regression, we invent false explanations for natural statistical pullback.

punchline.text: What goes up doesn't always come down — but extreme luck usually does.

quiz.question: What causes regression to the mean?
options: Extreme results partly reflect luck, and luck is unlikely to be equally extreme on the next measurement
People become less motivated after good performance
Tests become harder over time

hook.text: DNA matches the suspect with 1-in-a-million accuracy. Guilty? Not so fast — in a city of 8 million, expect 8 matches.

buildup.text: The fallacy confuses the probability of evidence given innocence with the probability of innocence given evidence.

discovery.text: P(match | innocent) = 0.000001. But P(innocent | match) depends on how many people were tested.

twist.text: Sally Clark was wrongly convicted partly due to this fallacy. A doctor misstated the probability in court.

climax.text: Bayes' theorem is the correct framework. The prior probability of guilt matters as much as the evidence.

punchline.text: Rare evidence doesn't mean rare innocence. The fallacy jails people.

quiz.question: What does the prosecutor's fallacy confuse?
options: The probability of evidence given innocence with the probability of innocence given evidence
The guilt of the suspect with the length of the sentence
DNA evidence with fingerprint evidence

hook.text: A test is 99% accurate. You test positive for a rare disease. The chance you actually have it? Maybe 10%.

buildup.text: If a disease affects 1 in 1,000 people, testing 10,000 gives 10 true positives and about 100 false positives.

discovery.text: Your chance of having the disease is 10 out of 110 positive results — about 9%. The base rate dominates.

twist.text: Doctors consistently overestimate positive predictive value. Even medical professionals fall for the base rate trap.

climax.text: Screening programs for rare conditions produce mostly false alarms. The math is counter-intuitive but unforgiving.

punchline.text: Accuracy means little when the condition is rare. Base rates rule everything.

quiz.question: Why does a 99% accurate test still produce mostly false positives for a rare disease?
options: When few people have the disease, the 1% false positive rate generates more positives than true cases
The test is actually broken
Rare diseases are never tested accurately

hook.text: A hospital is measured by wait times. Solution: treat easy patients first. Wait times drop — but outcomes worsen.

buildup.text: Goodhart's Law: when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure.

discovery.text: People optimize for the metric, not the goal behind it. The metric gets gamed while the real objective suffers.

twist.text: Soviet nail factories measured by weight made huge nails. Measured by count, they made millions of tiny ones.

climax.text: Every KPI, metric, and target in business and government is vulnerable to Goodhart's Law.

punchline.text: Measure it and people game it. The target corrupts the measure.

quiz.question: What does Goodhart's Law state?
options: When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure — people optimize the metric, not the goal
All measurements are inherently inaccurate
Targets always improve performance

hook.text: In Vietnam, the US measured success by body count. They were 'winning' by the numbers and losing the war.

buildup.text: Robert McNamara quantified everything measurable and ignored everything that wasn't. A fatal error.

discovery.text: The McNamara Fallacy: measuring only what's easy to count and assuming it captures the full picture.

twist.text: Body counts said the US was winning. Morale, political will, and guerrilla effectiveness said otherwise.

climax.text: The fallacy persists in business: measuring engagement clicks while ignoring customer satisfaction.

punchline.text: Not everything that counts can be counted. Not everything counted counts.

quiz.question: What is the McNamara Fallacy?
options: Measuring only what's easy to quantify and ignoring important factors that are hard to measure
Counting military casualties incorrectly
Using old data to make modern decisions

hook.text: A Texan fires randomly at a barn, then paints a target around the tightest cluster. Bullseye every time.

buildup.text: The Texas Sharpshooter fallacy is finding a pattern in random data after the fact and claiming it was predicted.

discovery.text: With enough data points, clusters appear by chance. Drawing conclusions from post-hoc patterns is circular reasoning.

twist.text: Cancer cluster investigations find this: residents identify a cluster, then search for a cause that may not exist.

climax.text: Pre-registration — stating your hypothesis before looking at data — is the defense against this fallacy.

punchline.text: Predict, then shoot. Don't shoot, then draw the target.

quiz.question: What is the Texas Sharpshooter fallacy?
options: Finding a pattern in random data after the fact and claiming it was predicted
Shooting at targets that are too close
Using a sample that is too small

hook.text: A man searches for his keys under a streetlight. 'Did you lose them here?' 'No, but the light is better.'

buildup.text: The streetlight effect: researchers study what's easy to measure, not necessarily what's most important.

discovery.text: Entire research fields can drift toward convenient data sources, leaving critical questions unstudied.

twist.text: Social media research exploded because data is freely available. Important questions with hard-to-get data get ignored.

climax.text: The effect explains gaps in medical research too: diseases with measurable biomarkers get studied more than vague ones.

punchline.text: We study where the data is, not where the answers are.

quiz.question: What is the streetlight effect in research?
options: Studying what's easy to measure rather than what's most important, due to data availability
Conducting research only at night
Focusing only on well-funded topics

hook.text: Schools are ranked by test scores. Teachers start teaching to the test. Scores rise. Education doesn't.

buildup.text: Campbell's Law: the more a quantitative indicator is used for decisions, the more subject to corruption it becomes.

discovery.text: High-stakes metrics incentivize gaming. Teachers narrow the curriculum. Hospitals refuse high-risk patients.

twist.text: Campbell's Law is the social science cousin of Goodhart's Law. Together, they warn against naive metric worship.

climax.text: The solution isn't no measurement — it's multiple measures, transparency, and awareness of gaming incentives.

punchline.text: High stakes + single metric = guaranteed gaming. Diversify your measures.

quiz.question: How does Campbell's Law relate to Goodhart's Law?
options: Both warn that high-stakes metrics get gamed — Campbell focuses on social indicators, Goodhart on economics
They are completely unrelated concepts
Campbell's Law says measurement is always accurate

hook.text: A supermarket displays 24 jam flavors. Another displays 6. The table with 6 sells ten times more. Why?

buildup.text: More choices should be better. But Sheena Iyengar's famous jam study showed that too many options paralyze decisions.

discovery.text: With 24 options, only 3% bought. With 6 options, 30% bought. Choice overload freezes people.

twist.text: Later replications found weaker effects. The paradox is real but context-dependent.

climax.text: The sweet spot exists between too few options (no fit) and too many (paralysis). Design matters.

punchline.text: More isn't always better. Sometimes less choice means more action.

quiz.question: What did Sheena Iyengar's jam study demonstrate?
options: Too many choices can paralyze decisions — fewer options led to ten times more purchases
People always prefer more variety
Jam flavor doesn't affect purchasing decisions

hook.text: Would you take a sure $1 million or a gamble with 10% chance of $5 million? Most take the sure thing.

buildup.text: Shift the scenario slightly. Most people's second choice contradicts their first — violating utility theory.

discovery.text: The Allais paradox shows people overweight certainty. The jump from 99% to 100% feels enormous.

twist.text: Expected utility predicts consistent choices. Humans aren't consistent — we avoid the regret of losing sure things.

climax.text: The paradox inspired Kahneman and Tversky's prospect theory — a more realistic model of how humans make risky decisions.

punchline.text: Certainty has a premium that probability can't explain. We're human.

quiz.question: What does the Allais paradox reveal about human decision-making?
options: People overweight certainty — the jump from 99% to 100% feels disproportionately valuable
People always choose the riskier option
Expected utility theory perfectly predicts human choices

hook.text: A coin is flipped until heads. Tails on flip 1 pays $2, flip 2 pays $4, and so on. Expected value: infinity.

buildup.text: The sum of payoff × probability diverges: $1 + $1 + $1 + ... = infinity. Rationally, you should pay any price to play.

discovery.text: Nobody would pay more than about $25. Infinite expected value meets finite human willingness to pay.

twist.text: Daniel Bernoulli resolved it in 1738: people value money logarithmically. Doubling wealth doesn't double happiness.

climax.text: The paradox launched utility theory and the idea that expected value alone doesn't drive decisions.

punchline.text: Infinite expected value, finite price. Utility, not money, drives decisions.

quiz.question: How did Daniel Bernoulli resolve the St. Petersburg paradox?
options: He proposed that people value money logarithmically — doubling wealth doesn't double satisfaction
He proved the expected value was actually finite
He showed the game could never actually be played

hook.text: A city builds a new road to reduce traffic. Congestion gets worse. Adding capacity made the network slower.

buildup.text: Braess's paradox: adding a shortcut to a network can slow everyone down when each driver acts selfishly.

discovery.text: Each driver takes the individually fastest route. But when everyone does, the shortcut becomes a bottleneck.

twist.text: Closing roads has actually improved traffic in cities. Seoul removed an elevated highway and traffic got better.

climax.text: The paradox occurs in electrical networks, internet routing, and basketball — adding a star player can slow the team.

punchline.text: More roads, more traffic. Individual optimization defeats collective good.

quiz.question: What causes Braess's paradox in traffic networks?
options: Each driver chooses the individually fastest route, but collectively this creates worse congestion
New roads are always built poorly
Traffic lights cause all congestion

hook.text: On average, your friends have more friends than you do. This is mathematically guaranteed, not just bad luck.

buildup.text: Popular people appear in many friend lists. You're more likely to be friends with someone who has many friends.

discovery.text: It's a sampling bias: high-degree nodes are overrepresented when you sample through connections.

twist.text: The paradox extends: your co-authors publish more, your partners have more exes, your followers have more followers.

climax.text: It's used in public health: vaccinating friends of random people targets well-connected individuals efficiently.

punchline.text: Your friends are more popular than you. It's not personal — it's math.

quiz.question: Why do your friends tend to have more friends than you?
options: Popular people appear in many friend lists, so they're overrepresented in your sample of friends
You don't have enough friends
Social media inflates friend counts

hook.text: In 2015, scientists tried to replicate 100 psychology studies. Only 36 produced the same result.

buildup.text: The replication crisis revealed that many published findings were false positives from noisy data.

discovery.text: P-hacking, small samples, publication bias, and researcher degrees of freedom all inflated the false discovery rate.

twist.text: The crisis isn't limited to psychology. Medicine, economics, and biology all face similar problems.

climax.text: Pre-registration, larger samples, and open data are improving research quality. Science is self-correcting — slowly.

punchline.text: Most published findings might be wrong. The crisis made science more honest.

quiz.question: What was the main finding of the 2015 replication study in psychology?
options: Only 36 out of 100 studies produced the same result when replicated
All studies replicated perfectly
Replication is unnecessary in science

hook.text: Overweight heart failure patients live longer than normal-weight ones. Does being overweight protect you?

buildup.text: Multiple studies found that moderate obesity was associated with better survival in heart failure and other diseases.

discovery.text: The paradox is likely selection bias: normal-weight patients with heart failure may have more aggressive disease.

twist.text: Collider bias, reverse causation, and weight measurement timing all contribute. The paradox likely isn't real.

climax.text: It's a masterclass in how observational data can produce paradoxical, misleading associations.

punchline.text: The data says overweight helps. The biology says otherwise. Trust the mechanism.

quiz.question: What likely explains the obesity paradox in heart failure patients?
options: Selection and collider bias — normal-weight patients with heart failure may have more aggressive disease
Being overweight genuinely protects the heart
The studies used incorrect weight measurements

topics.title: Benford's Law: The First Digit Paradox

hook.text: In financial records, the digit 1 appears first about 30% of the time. Not 11% as you'd expect. Why?

buildup.text: Benford's Law predicts that lower digits appear as leading digits far more often than higher ones.

discovery.text: Numbers grow multiplicatively. Going from 1 to 2 is 100% increase; 8 to 9 is just 12.5%.

twist.text: Forensic accountants use Benford's Law to detect fraud. Fabricated numbers don't follow the natural pattern.

climax.text: The law applies to city populations, river lengths, stock prices, and elections. It's remarkably universal.

punchline.text: Nature favors small first digits. Fake data doesn't know that.

quiz.question: Why does the digit 1 appear as a leading digit about 30% of the time?
options: Numbers grow multiplicatively — it takes a 100% increase to go from 1 to 2, so more numbers start with 1
It's a coincidence in small datasets
Number systems are designed to favor 1

hook.text: On average, the next bus arrives in 10 minutes. You've waited 15. Expected remaining wait? Still 10 minutes.

buildup.text: If bus arrivals follow an exponential distribution, the process is memoryless. Past waiting doesn't change the future.

discovery.text: The probability of waiting another 10 minutes is the same whether you just arrived or have waited an hour.

twist.text: Real buses follow schedules. But many natural processes are memoryless: radioactive decay, server requests.

climax.text: The exponential is the only continuous memoryless distribution. This fact has deep consequences.

punchline.text: The bus doesn't know you waited. Every moment starts fresh.

quiz.question: What does 'memoryless' mean for the exponential distribution?
options: Past waiting time doesn't change the probability distribution of remaining wait time
The process has no data recorded about it
Each event is exactly the same duration

hook.text: How long is Britain's coastline? It depends on the length of your ruler. Shorter rulers give longer coastlines.

buildup.text: Benoit Mandelbrot showed that coastlines are fractals: the measured length increases as measurement precision increases.

discovery.text: With a 100 km ruler, Britain's coast is about 2,800 km. With a 50 km ruler, it's 3,400 km. It never converges.

twist.text: The fractal dimension quantifies roughness. Britain's coast is about 1.25 — between a line and a plane.

climax.text: The paradox extends beyond geography: financial markets, tree branching, and blood vessels are all fractal.

punchline.text: Zoom in and there's always more detail. Some lengths have no answer.

quiz.question: Why does a coastline's measured length increase with shorter rulers?
options: Shorter rulers capture more detail in the jagged coastline — it's fractal, so length never converges
Shorter rulers are less accurate
Tides change the coastline between measurements
